speaker: interviewer
text: You did not leave with the British. So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Dresden to cross the river. Because they said we had to cross the river to go to Prague. I wanted to go to Prague. And before the bridge, I met a group of people, girls. And with them, together, we didn't cross the bridge. We never did because the directions was the other-- the way I came. And we were from the Czech border around 30 kilometers. But we have to take other ways. There was no bridges.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian-- did you know any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I got to know them. And we stick together the whole way. We had a very good communication. And we were together all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was policing that area? Were there any-- were there Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans? Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody-- it was no-man's land.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-- it's no-man's-- I saw British soldiers. I thought British, because they had a different uniform than the Russians. So they must be British. And this was East German, right? Nobody-- it was no land. But the whole Dresden looked horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay with these girls? And where did-- what did you eat? And how did you manage to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had food. The Russians-- the Russians put a bullet into a cow. And everybody was taking pieces of meat. And they gave us pieces of meat, too. And we went into a house. And we cooked it. And we ate it. We slept over. And the next day, we walk, walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your goal? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to get-- all of you wanted to go to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us. We met another group of men, too. Because the men had to protect us from the Russians, not from the Germans anymore, from the Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the Russians doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Raping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't care. No, no I meant in general, did they know that you had been in camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because, because I met a Russian officer. And he promised me to take me to Prague by his truck. And I told him, I'm Jewish. He says, I'm Jewish, too. And I was so happy. And he gave me some apples to eat. And then before he take me to the truck, he wanted to rape me, the Jewish officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it didn't matter to them if you are Jewish or not. The main thing, we're a girl. We're skinny, 90 pounds, it doesn't matter, either. I didn't understood it then. But I understand now that when a soldier is on-- in the war, he's hungry for a woman. And he doesn't care who she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I said to the soldier, I'm a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Why don't you take German girls? Why me? And he said, if you don't shut up, I'll shoot you and put the gun close to the head, the Russian soldier. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I-- then I fainted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. How did you get to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked to the Czech border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get across the Czech border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing-- it wasn't a border at all. It wasn't a border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your goal to get away from the Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't get away from the Russians. Because they were all over. They were in Europe, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Prague under Russian rule at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right-- whole Europe and whole Germany was under the Russians. We didn't get away from them, it-- not for one minute for the next few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reconnect with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is interesting. How did I reconnect? I came to Prague. Prague was offering me to stay in a hotel as long as I want to, with a beautiful room. They turned the hotels into refugee homes for refugees, with food and with rooms and with clothing and with everything, and long as--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was supporting this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The joint American money.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint, the Joint, JDC.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I stayed for four days. And I looked through the walls if I find someone. And let's go. Let's go home. And it took me a long time hitchhiking. Trains weren't going. Or a train, if it went for two hours, then the Russians took away the locomotive. And we were stuck the middle of nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you still with the same, with these same girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. In Prague, we separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another group-- big deal, another group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch at all with the Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague. And how did you do that? How did-- did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reach them or they reached you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They reached me. They reached me, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And

speaker: interviewee
text: On the station-- when I came into Prague with a, with a train, they reached me. And they put me to the hotel. And they told me, you can stay as long as you want to. Yeah. You know, I stayed four days. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was their goal to convince you to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their goal?

speaker: interviewee
text: To feed me, to build me up-- this was their goal. We were not talking about Palestine at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go from Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Prague, I went to Bratislava-- the same thing, stayed in a hotel-- from Bratislava to Kosice. Kosice, I stayed in a private house. And the closer I got home, I became more depressed. I was afraid what that I have nobody. And in Kosice, I was very depressed. And a young man came into the house every day and just sat and looked at me. I didn't know why. Now I'm thinking why. Then I didn't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: After a few days, he asked me, what's your name? And I said my name. Where are you from? And he sits. And  he says, you know, I saw your brother. And I jumped up. Where did you see my brother? He said, at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stay another minute. I packed my bag and left right away and just left for the woman who I lived with a note. I am leaving. And I went, hitchhiking still because the trains didn't go and buses didn't go. The bridges were down. It took me some two weeks-- it, it wasn't a big distance-- till I got home into our town on a beer truck, a truck who was delivering beer. And I came in at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're talking about Svalava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What was it called then? Was it Svalava? Or did it have the Hungarian name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Soivo. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Soi-- mhm. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I jumped down from the running truck. And it was, it was so dark, not a person in the street. I don't know if it was my soul dark or really dark, or I saw a dark. I don't know. But I didn't see a person and just start to run towards the houses. And a soldier is running this way. And this was my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we hugged. And you know what he asked, the first word? Where's my mother? So I says to him, you don't know? No, I want to hear it from you. I said, mother is not alive anymore. How did she die? I said, she was burned in Auschwitz. Where is sister? I said, the same way. And he turned around. And he started to cry and hitting the wall. Which brother is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eliezer, yeah. And when he calmed down, just then, he take a look at me. He didn't see me and-- because his first thought was where he knew that he has nobody. But he had to ask. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know you were alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He heard about it, that I'm on my way. And that's why he stayed waiting for me. He didn't know what to do. If he go look for me, we might cross each other. So he was staying and waiting for me. And then he takes a look at me. And he says, you are so skinny. By then, I gained weight already. I wasn't that skinny. And he said, don't worry. I'll take care of you. And he did. Till this day, he is taking care of me. But all my problems, I go to him. And he solves them. He's very smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remain in Svalava any length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and then, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Communists came. But I, I left before the Communists took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave with your brother or--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another-- you have another brother, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we met him later. They came home later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know he was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just heard it from someone. And then we met them later in, in Prague. One was in a hospital, very sick. And one was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many survived in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four. Who was the fourth one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The three brothers and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had three brothers and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And where did you go then after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did we go? We went from one soup kitchen to the other. [INTERPOSING VOICES] This was our-- [? often-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not in a, in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: The DP, the DP camps were in Germany. And I didn't want to go back to Germany no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get out of Communist country?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we get out? To Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Legally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but how did you go? From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Teplice-Sanov.

speaker: interviewer
text: In where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teplice-Sanov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia. And I had a baby. I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Teplice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sanov.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czechoslovakia and you had a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the Israeli-- Israel was just-- you know, how you say-- proclaimed?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948-- after the proclamation, we went to Israel legally with papers by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where? From--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia to Italy by train and from Italy with a ship to Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Communists let you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just became Communist country for a few weeks. They let out. One of my brothers stuck there. He wanted to go out later. He couldn't get out for 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: He remained in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went, you went from Italy. Where in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Napoli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Napoli. And, and who took you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sochnut.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Sochnut, to Haifa and then to Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You went as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were did you settle in, in-- what happened when you got to Palestine-- or to Israel? At that point, it was Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in Jerusalem for 15 years, an Arab house. The Arabs were chased away. And they put a family in one room. And the Arabs built their houses-- they had one big hall. And every room had a family. And the man was the husband for all the wives and one kitchen. When they were chased away-- and they had very big houses, beautiful houses-- the Sochnut put a, every family in a room. If it was four bedrooms, it's four families. And if it's three bedrooms, three families. And all three families had to use the one kitchen and the one bathroom and the one toilet. So you were standing in line to go to the toilet. And when the kids had to go to school in the morning, you had 20 kids to go to the toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was hard times-- again, no food. Everything was rationed. The men went away to the army. My husband was in the army for two years. And every year, they called the men for retraining. And it was a hard life, a hard life in Israel. OK? Your-- what happened to your, your child, your husband and child? You said you were divorced.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they go to Israel with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I divorced in Israel. And then I married the recent husband two and a half years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your child is with you or with--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was with me. But she-- this is Eva. She married at age 20 here in New York. She took an American guy from Kew Gardens. And she went back to Israel. She doesn't want to live here. She likes it there. And she lives in Jerusalem. And she has four kids. And she has a happy marriage and a happy family. And they make very well. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were divorced and you remarried in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel. When did you make a decision to come to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I lived in this one room with three children, then I made the decision that I can't take it anymore. I had a 15-year-old girl. And I had no way of getting an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a waitress. I couldn't work much. My children were always sick. They were never healthy and nothing serious-- a cold, an earache, a throat ache. But I was going to the [HEBREW SPEECH]. This is the clinic. It didn't go through a week I shouldn't go to [HEBREW SPEECH]. I don't know why. Later, I figured out we used-- we had mosquitoes. We used DDT. And we were spritzing in the house with the baby in the room. It could be they got sick from this. But I didn't know it at the time. And we didn't have the proper food. I didn't know about nutrition. And they didn't have it. We were standing in line to get some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any family in, in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always together. We were always together. We never separated. We never separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made you-- when did you decide to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: All my brothers came before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to. But when they are here and I have such a hard life, so I came to-- this, this was the Promised Land.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they bring you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much do your children know about your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of them want to know. It's very interesting. The daughter in Israel, the oldest one, she is dying to know. She was nagging me all those year. [? Ima, ?] talk. I want to know. I said, take a book and read. No, I want your story. I couldn't do it. She was sitting with her children around the table. And she said, the children have to make a project for school about the Holocaust. And I looked at the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can I tell them those stories? I just can't. I can't. I couldn't. And now, I've decided, yes. Do you think that your children at some point knew, anyhow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure, they did. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they-- did they ask you questions, the other children, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have three children, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, they don't ask questions. If I volunteer something to say to the second daughter, she's very interested. But she wouldn't ask. And my son doesn't want to know. He doesn't want to hear about it. But I read a book about it that children like this, they don't want to know about it. Because it's very painful for them. He doesn't want to hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandchildren? They're not little anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they are interested. But they wouldn't ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't. And you have not volunteered?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime, and they are interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your husband a survivor, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not like me. He was in the force, in this labor force.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Hungarian labor force?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I have to continue with my family. We are 16 people now, just me, just from me. That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: What helped you most during the war year, during that period from 1944 through-- till liberation or after liberation? What helped you most?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think because I was alone. And I couldn't rely on nobody. I have to do it on my own. That's what helped me. I was strong, it looks like. But then, after the war, I had nervous breakdowns, many. I didn't have a good life. I was sick-- nerves, migraine headaches. And now, I'm older, when I'm getting older, getting much, much better. Because I understand more. I read about it a lot. I know. And I am very proud of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the thing that you were most afraid of during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't afraid at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about this period in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I dream? Always-- it never leaves me, not just dream at night when I sleep, during the day, too. The whole life is connected to this. My whole life, I see everything through this.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this country, your brothers were already here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- were they the ones who helped you here? Or did you get help from other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- no, the brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the garment center-- I worked in the garment center all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: As well-- he's a tailor, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, if any, impact did your experiences have in raising your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: A tremendous impact-- they feel it, second generation. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way do you think they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't think like any other children. I have my daughters. Their friends are just Holocaust survivors' kids, my friends, just Holocaust survivors. I have nothing in common with any other people. And they don't, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you raise them in a specific way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That the way you raised them had something to do with the way-- what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way? How would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would never, ever let then put a piece of bread in the garbage, ever. Even if it's two weeks old, I'll eat it. And if I see them do it, they're hiding it if they want to throw out a piece of bread. And if they do something I don't like it, I was very strict with them. So I said, I didn't survive the camps I should see this you doing. You'll stop it right now. And it did stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see the way your children are raising their own children? Is there-- is that too similar, as well? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I think they're raising them well. But it must have an impact on them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add to what you have told us before we show some of your pictures and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about the restitution. Some people get restitution from the Germans, from the Germans for the past 30 years, 35 years. And it's a very big help. They can afford from this money take vacations, to give presents for the kids, for the grandchildren. I don't do this, because I cannot afford it. And I wouldn't take from the Germans the restitution. Because what they did to my parents, I feel that they cannot pay me off. And-- but last year, I filled out the papers for restitution. And I just started to get now in August my first check of 500 marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You get it in marks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they send it straight to the bank. And I got it in dollars. And it's around $360. I need the money, because my husband is a sick man. And he takes very expensive medication, very expensive. I don't feel like spending the money. I'm getting it now. But I don't feel like spending it. And I don't know. I would give it away if he would let me. He won't let me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for sharing your story with us. And we are going to show some of the pictures you have. And when your husband gets back and your son gets here-- or your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have them on the tape as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, if there's something else you wish to add before we close the tape, please do so.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'll feel much better I got it off my heart. I have more, much more details to tell. But this is little things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Klein.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
